segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
For more than a thousand years, Constantinople stood where Europe met Asia.

A city of merchants.

A city of emperors.

A city protected by walls so formidable that generation after generation believed they could never fall.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text: History loves hearing people say "never."

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: How big was the walls?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
The city had survived invasions, rebellions, and sieges.

Empires had risen and disappeared while Constantinople remained.

To its citizens, the walls were more than stone.

They were certainty.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text: And certainty can be dangerous.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: What happened to the history?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
In 1453, a young Ottoman sultan named Mehmed looked upon the city.

He was only twenty-one years old.

Most saw a ruler.

He saw a challenge.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text: Or an opportunity.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: What does the fall look like?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
Beyond those walls stood one of the most important cities in the world.

A prize many had attempted to claim.

A prize none had managed to keep.

So Mehmed prepared carefully.

Patiently.

Relentlessly.

The defenders watched from the walls.

Confident.

After all, the walls had always held.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text: The walls had never met gunpowder like this.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: How big was the years?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
Then the cannons began to fire.

The earth shook.

Stone cracked.

Dust filled the air.

Day after day, the bombardment continued.

The defenders repaired the damage each night.

The attackers returned each morning.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text: A contest between stone and time.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: Where did the mehmed go?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
Yet even that was not enough.

Because Mehmed had another plan.

The city's harbor was protected by a giant chain stretched across the water.

It seemed impossible to bypass.

So he didn't bypass it.

He went around it.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text:
Not around the harbor.

Around reality's expectations.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: How did the stone...?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
His men dragged ships across land.

Across hills.

Across wooden tracks.

When the people of Constantinople awoke, enemy ships were suddenly inside waters they believed were safe.

The city was no longer surrounded on one side.

It was surrounded everywhere.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text: And that changes how a city sleeps.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: Where did the across go?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
Weeks passed.

Food dwindled.

Exhaustion spread.

Every repaired wall became another wall to defend.

Every victory became harder than the last.

Then came the morning of May 29.

The final assault.

Wave after wave surged toward the breaches.

The defenders fought with extraordinary courage.

But even legends have limits.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text: And every wall meets its final day.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: What does the city look like?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
By the end of that day, Constantinople had fallen.

An empire more than a thousand years old came to an end.

The last continuation of ancient Rome disappeared into history.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text:
Not with a single moment.

But with centuries leading to it.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: How did the people...?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
Because cities rarely fall in a day.

Empires rarely fall in a battle.

The final chapter is simply the one people remember.

segments.speaker: Voice 2
segments.text:
The story was ending.

A different one was beginning.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: What happened to the constantinople?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text:
And as the sun set over Constantinople, the world looked much the same.

The streets still existed.

The sea still touched the shore.

The sky remained unchanged.

Yet history had quietly crossed a boundary.

One age was over.

Another had begun.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text:
I liked the story.
I didn't understand all of it.
But I think I understood the end.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: Do you think the city is still out there somewhere?
